label: ai
topic: An alien scholar in an underground cave society must undertake a pilgrimage to eat
text:
The limestone was cold, but the words carved into it pulsed with a phantom warmth beneath Kharatet's sensitive fingertips. He traced the deep, jagged grooves of the ancestral dialect, his mind translating the tactile bumps into the rhythmic cadence of the Deep Current poetry. His blind eyes, pale and clouded like the surface of a frozen puddle, stared straight ahead into the darkness of the cavern. Around him, the hatchlings clicked their nascent claws against the damp floor, a chorus of eager, chitinous castanets. Kharatet flared his neck gills, drawing in the humid, sulfur-rich air of the subterranean colony, tasting the metallic tang of the nearby thermal vents.

"The water boils, the stone bleeds," Kharatet recited, his voice a low, resonant hum that vibrated in his throat sac. "The lightless world provides the seed."

A sudden, violent spasm wracked his abdomen. He hunched over, his long, prehensile tail curling tightly around his ankles to steady himself. Beneath his translucent, pale blue skin, the dark, shriveled mass of his empty stomach contracted visibly. The hatchlings ceased their clicking, sensing his distress through the subtle shifts in the cavern's atmospheric pressure. Kharatet forced a calming pheromone from his dorsal glands, hoping to soothe them, but the gnawing ache in his gut was becoming difficult to mask.

"Elder brother," a voice echoed from the tunnel entrance. It was Xiri, her footsteps heavy, the suction pads on her toes squelching softly against the wet stone. She carried the faint, bitter scent of the upper fungal forests.

Kharatet straightened his spine, attempting to look dignified despite the hollow cavern of his torso. "I am in the midst of a recitation, Xiri. The young ones must learn the verses of the First Molt."

"The poetry will have to wait," Xiri said gently, stepping into the circle of hatchlings. She placed a cool, webbed hand on Kharatet's trembling shoulder. Through her translucent skin, he knew, her organs would be plump and vibrant purple, nourished by the fruits of the Great Descent. "You are starving, Kharatet. Your bioluminescence is entirely faded. You look like a ghost of the upper world."

"I am a scholar," Kharatet replied stubbornly, though his voice betrayed him with a weak rasp. "The mind feeds on history. The body is secondary."

"The body requires the deep-sea lichen, and you know it," she chided, her tone maternal but firm. "You have refused the pilgrimage for three cycles. You sit here in the nursery tunnels, reading stones, while your own flesh consumes itself."

The pilgrimage, the Great Descent, required swimming down through the crushing pressure of the abyssal trenches to harvest the bioluminescent flora that grew only on the corpses of leviathans. It was a brutal, physical ordeal, one that Kharatet found deeply uncivilized. He preferred the quiet permanence of the archives, the dusty scent of pulverized rock, the safety of the shallow pools.

"The trenches are for hunters," Kharatet muttered, his gills fluttering in agitation. "I am a keeper of the word."

"If you die of starvation, who will read to them?" Xiri asked, gesturing to the hatchlings who were now chittering softly, rubbing their soft muzzles against Kharatet's shins. "The stones do not speak on their own, brother. They require breath to give them voice. And you are running out of breath."

Another hunger pang hit him, sharp as a volcanic glass shard. He gasped, his knees buckling slightly. Xiri caught his arm, her grip surprisingly strong. She did not offer him pity, only the pragmatic truth of their biology. We are creatures of the dark water, her grip seemed to say. We must eat of the dark water to survive.

Kharatet ran his fingers over the carved limestone one last time, lingering on the word for endurance. He sighed, a wet, rattling sound, and uncurled his tail. "Very well, sister," he whispered, turning his blind eyes toward the echoing roar of the deep trenches. "Show me the way down."
